headline: GRAINS DRIFT LOWER ON WELCOME RAINS
subheadline: Belt moisture ends dry spell, removes weather premium from July contracts.
lead: Corn closed $4.41, down a half-cent, as welcome rains across the middle Mississippi Valley removed the last weather premium from July contracts. The Belt got its first meaningful moisture in two weeks and temperatures moderated to seasonal warmth, exactly what pollinating corn needed. Funds liquidated another 8,000 grain contracts Monday, finishing the Memorial cleanup that started last week.
the_takeaway: Weather premium gone, funds finishing cleanup, summer range taking shape.
teaser: Belt rains kill weather premium, funds finish Memorial liquidation.

one_number.unit: corn planting completion as of May 24
one_number.context: The planting season that looked behind schedule through early May caught up between rain systems. Belt farmers pushed through 42% completion by Memorial Day, ahead of the 5-year average despite the wet start. Now welcome rains are hitting at exactly the right time for pollination, not planting pressure.

yesterdays_call.summary: Geopolitical premium back after three weeks of unwinding in energy markets.
yesterdays_call.note: Crude gave back yesterday's Iran move higher, premium deflating again on diplomatic progress.

sections.title: GRAINS EASE ON BELT MOISTURE
sections.body: Corn lost a half-cent to $4.41 as the first meaningful **Belt rains in two weeks** hit the middle Mississippi Valley. Soybeans dropped 12¾ cents to $11.66, the biggest decline in the complex. Chicago wheat eased 3½ cents to $6.01. The moisture couldn't have timed better for pollinating corn, removing the weather premium that built through late May. Fund liquidation continues from Memorial week, another 8,000 contracts cleared Monday according to trader estimates.
sections.catalyst: Welcome rains across Plains and Mississippi Valley end two-week dry spell
sections.bottom_line: Weather premium gone, funds cleaning books, July contracts finding summer floor.

sections.title: CATTLE DRIFT ON LIGHT VOLUME
sections.body: Live cattle closed $238.70, down $1.65 in thin trade. Feeders lost $3.50 to $347.60, the ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began Tuesday May 19 still pressuring the complex. No fresh news on the **1,700-worker lockout** that's removed roughly 6,000 head daily from processing capacity. Box-beef cutout held steady, no catalyst for directional moves. Cash trade hasn't reset since the last disruption.
sections.bottom_line: Processing disruption narrative unchanged, no catalyst for breakout moves.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC launches antitrust probe into fertilizer pricing
outside_the_pit.body: The Federal Trade Commission confirmed an investigation into rising fertilizer costs, the agency head announced Thursday. Input costs that stayed elevated through spring planting season now face regulatory scrutiny for potential anti-competitive practices.

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm found 31 miles from US border
outside_the_pit.body: The parasitic fly that eats warm-blooded animals alive was detected just south of the border. Could cause millions in economic damage if it crosses into US livestock operations, triggering enhanced border monitoring protocols.

outside_the_pit.title: Missouri agronomist warns heavy rains fuel nitrogen loss
outside_the_pit.body: Recent rainfall timing creates challenges for crop nutrition and weed control, according to Cropwise Consulting. Excess moisture leaching nitrogen from fields while fueling weed pressure, forcing replanning of sidedress applications.

spread_to_watch.label: July corn / December corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: The carry trade stayed locked through Memorial liquidation, telling you the funds aren't panicking out of new-crop positions. December corn at $4.67 versus July at $4.41 prices normal harvest pressure, not weather fear. When the carry starts moving, the weather story changes.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming on ethanol restart
basis.body: **Ethanol plants coming back online** after maintenance is tightening corn basis east of the Mississippi. Producers with old-crop bushels in storage have a window the futures alone aren't pricing. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with seasonal patterns as harvest approaches.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain liquidation finish before weather takes control of the story?
weekly_thread.status_text: Liquidation's still the driver. Monday's fund selling cleared another 8,000 contracts while Belt rains removed weather premium, suggesting the cleanup has more room to run before July weather becomes the story.

the_more_you_know.title: Modern corn makes 'knee-high by July 4th' obsolete
the_more_you_know.body: Today's corn at 42% planted is already outpacing the old timing rules. A Wisconsin farmer notes modern genetics and fertility have corn growing faster than the traditional "knee-high by July 4th" benchmark. Current hybrids routinely hit shoulder-high by Independence Day in good conditions. The old sayings price weather risk from a different era of genetics. Today's corn handles stress better but moves faster through growth stages, compressing the critical pollination window that drives July weather premiums.

watch_list.desc: Weekly export sales; beans under 300K MT keeps chart in charge
Belt temps above 90°F for 3+ days adds pollination stress premium

daily_quote.text: Lost time is never found again.

source_summary: USDA NASS, CME Group, Brownfield Ag News, Feedstuffs
